prompt:
progress.py is the one-job-one-line bar our deploy tool prints ([####----] style, injectable width, floored percent). The tool now runs steps in parallel and the single bar is useless, so this sprint's ticket is three things, and I have opinions about all of them.

(1) MultiBar(width=24, clock=None). add(label, total) registers a job and hands back its ProgressBar; a duplicate label is a ValueError. render(show_eta=False) returns a LIST of lines, one per job, always in the order the jobs were added — I don't care which bar updated last, reordering bars mid-run is how you make people seasick — with each line being the label left-justified to the widest label, one space, then that bar's own render. Empty MultiBar renders [].

(2) ETA that doesn't jump around. ProgressBar grows a clock kwarg (a callable returning seconds; default time.monotonic, and MultiBar hands its own clock down to the bars it creates). Construction stamps the start time. Each advance of n>0 units is timed against the previous advance (or construction): that's a rate sample of n/elapsed, and samples with zero elapsed time are worthless — drop them. Smooth with an EMA: first sample IS the rate, after that rate = 0.5*sample + 0.5*previous. eta() returns remaining/rate in seconds, None until we have a sample, 0.0 once the bar is done. render(show_eta=True) appends ' ETA M:SS' — round to the nearest whole second, minutes unpadded, seconds two digits, so 90s is 1:30 — and appends nothing when the ETA is unknown or the bar is done. Plain render() output must not change, ever; scripts parse it.

(3) summary() on MultiBar, for the CI log where 6 repainting bars are noise: one line, '<label> <pct>%' per job joined with ' | ', then ' >> <overall>%' where overall is floor(100*sum(current)/sum(total)) across all jobs (100 if the grand total is 0), then ' ETA M:SS' using the LARGEST eta among unfinished bars that have one — omit the ETA part if no unfinished bar has an eta.

Existing single-bar behavior (clamping, flooring, zero-total bar born done, exact line format) is load-bearing. test_progress.py is the acceptance contract — python3 test_progress.py — and the tests stay as they are.
